summary: Yegge argues that AI coding tools genuinely deliver massive productivity gains, but this creates a dangerous 'vampire' effect where companies extract all the value while workers burn out. He frames the problem as a value capture equation: if employees work 8 hours at 10x productivity, the company wins and the employee is drained; if employees slack off, the company dies. He takes accountability for setting unrealistic standards as an early adopter and warns that AI-native startups are poisoning the well by sprinting toward mostly terrible ideas. His proposed solution is the $/hr formula he invented at Amazon: you can't control your salary, but you can control your hours. He advocates for a 3-4 hour workday as the sustainable norm for AI-augmented knowledge work.
key_points: AI productivity gains are real but the critical question is who captures the value — if companies take 100%, workers burn out; if workers take 100%, companies fail
Agentic coding is genuinely addictive, doling out dopamine like a slot machine, and early adopters like Yegge are setting unrealistic beauty standards for normal developers
AI-native startups are the worst offenders, throwing talent at the same six tired ideas in a gold rush that will end in mass exhaustion without results
The $/hr formula from Amazon days applies: you can't control the numerator (salary) but you control the denominator (hours worked)
The sustainable AI workday should be 3-4 hours of intense decision-making, because AI has automated the easy work and left humans with only the exhausting parts
money_quotes: AI is starting to kill us all, Colin Robinson style.
You're damned if you do (you'll be drained) and you're damned if you don't (you'll be left behind.)
Companies are straight-up designed for extraction, and so you need to be the counter-force.
Someone else might control the numerator. But you control the denominator.
We're all setting unrealistic standards for everyone else.
There's a massive amount of talent being thrown at an incredible dearth of real ideas, basically the same six tired pitches.
AI has turned us all into Jeff Bezos, by automating the easy work, and leaving us with all the difficult decisions, summaries, and problem-solving.
It's not even remotely sustainable for companies to capture 100% of the value from AI.
key_insight: AI's productivity gains are real, but without consciously fighting the 'vampire' extraction of those gains by employers, the result will be widespread burnout rather than shared prosperity — the sustainable answer is a dramatically shorter workday.
